html:
COMMISSION IMPLEMENTING REGULATION (EU) 2026/1373

on the imposition of a definitive anti-dumping duty and the final collection of the provisional duty introduced on imports of 1,4-butanediol originating in the People's Republic of China, Saudi Arabia, and the United States of America.

THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION HAS ADOPTED THIS REGULATION,

having regard to the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union,

having regard to Regulation (EU) 2016/1036 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 8 June 2016 on protection against dumped imports from countries not members of the European Union (hereinafter referred to as the "basic regulation"), in particular Article 9.4, and

On 6 June 2025, the European Commission (the "Commission") initiated an anti-dumping investigation concerning imports of 1,4-butanediol originating in the People's Republic of China (hereinafter "China"), the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia (hereinafter "Saudi Arabia"), and the United States of America (China, Saudi Arabia, and the United States of America are hereinafter jointly referred to as the "subject countries") pursuant to Article 5 of the basic regulation. The Commission published a notice of initiation in the Official Journal of the European Union (hereinafter "the notice of initiation").

The Commission initiated the investigation following a complaint lodged on 24 April 2025 by INEOS Solvents SA (the "complainant" or "INEOS"). The complaint was lodged by an industry within the Union concerned by the dumping, as referred to in Article 5.4 of the basic regulation. The complaint contained evidence of dumping and resulting material injury, which was sufficient to justify the initiation of an investigation.

The Commission made imports of the product in question subject to registration by Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/1718 (hereinafter "the registration regulation").

On 5 February 2026, the Commission imposed provisional anti-dumping duties on imports of 1,4-butanediol originating in the subject countries by Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2026/270 (hereinafter "the provisional duty regulation").

Following the disclosure of the key circumstances and considerations underlying the imposition of a provisional anti-dumping duty (the preliminary disclosure of information), Wanhua Chemical (Sichuan), Ltd, together with its affiliated companies (Wanhua Group), International Diol Company (IDC), Sipchem Marketing Company (SMC), and Sipchem Europe S.A. (SESA) (the exporting producer and affiliated companies in Saudi Arabia, collectively referred to as Sipchem), Lyondell Chemical Company (LCC), and Lyondell Chemie Nederland B.V. (LCN) (the exporting producer in the United States and its affiliated EU producer, collectively referred to as LYB), Envalior BV and Envalior GmbH (Envalior), Lubrizol Advanced Materials Europe BVBA (Lubrizol), INEOS, Novamont S.p.A (Novamont), and BASF SE (BASF), submitted written submissions expressing their views on the preliminary investigation findings, within the time limit specified in Article 2.1 of the regulation on provisional duties.

The parties that requested to be heard were given the opportunity to do so. Wanhua Group, Sipchem, and LYB were heard.

The Commission continued to gather and verify all information that it considered necessary for its final decisions. In order to reach the final decisions, the Commission took into account the views of the parties concerned and modified its preliminary conclusions where appropriate.

The Commission informed all parties concerned of the key circumstances and considerations on the basis of which it intended to impose a definitive anti-dumping duty on imports of 1,4-butanediol originating in the countries concerned (the final disclosure of information). All parties were given a time limit within which they could submit comments on the final disclosure of information. Comments were received from Wanhua Group, Sipchem, INEOS, and Covestro.

The parties that requested to be heard were given the opportunity to do so. Sipchem was heard.

In addition to the parties mentioned in the provisional tariff regulation, Lubrizol (a user of 1,4-butanediol) declared itself to be an interested party in the final stage of the investigation.

After the preliminary disclosure of information, LCN and Sipchem submitted further comments regarding the degree of support for the complaint (the analysis of representativeness).

Specifically, LCN repeated several arguments that were discussed in paragraphs 11 and 12 of the provisional tariff regulation, namely that the Commission had disregarded the lack of support for initiating the investigation into imports from the United States and Saudi Arabia, because LCN had expressed a "neutral" position regarding the investigation, stating that it had "no objections to imports from Saudi Arabia and the United States" while being "in favor of an investigation into imports originating in China." LCN also claimed that the fact that LCN had opposed the initiation had been confirmed in paragraph 411 of the provisional tariff regulation, and that the Commission had "deliberately ignored" the lack of representativeness of the complaint regarding the initiation of the investigation into imports of 1,4-butanediol from the United States. The Commission acknowledged that LCN had submitted comments regarding its opposition to the initiation of the investigation into the United States and Saudi Arabia after the sampling procedure had been completed. However, as stated in paragraph 14 of the provisional tariff regulation, support for the complaint was established through the analysis of representativeness on June 2, 2025, because no producer had raised objections, and one producer had taken a neutral position. The Commission found that the conditions of Article 5.4 of the basic regulation were met. These claims were therefore rejected.

Sipchem reiterated its position, as summarized in reason 12 of the provisional tariff regulation, stating that the investigation should be discontinued regarding imports of 1,4 butanediol from Saudi Arabia and the United States. This is because the Local Content Notification (LCN) had been forced to select an option in a checkbox and chose "neutral." Furthermore, considering that the LCN had elaborated on its views in the comment section of the checkbox (5), the Commission should have investigated more thoroughly and not solely relied on the "neutral" response, which did not reflect the additional views submitted for the analysis of representativeness. Sipchem argued that the Commission could have then confirmed that the LCN did not support the initiation of an investigation regarding imports from Saudi Arabia and the United States.

Additionally, in reason 303 of the provisional tariff regulation, Sipchem claimed that the Commission had determined that Chinese producers could set prices in the EU market, and that Sipchem was therefore a price taker. According to Sipchem, this is the reason why the LCN did not support the investigation of Saudi Arabia and the United States, and therefore the investigation should be discontinued with respect to these countries.

The Commission noted that Sipchem has not provided any evidence to support the claim that the LCN does not support an investigation of the United States or Saudi Arabia, because Sipchem allegedly does not itself set prices in the EU market. In contrast to these claims, as stated in reason 14 of...
tldr: The EU is introducing an additional tariff on the chemical 1,4-butanediol imported from China, Saudi Arabia, and the United States. This measure is being implemented to protect European companies from prices that are artificially low due to a practice known as dumping.
lattlast:
The European Commission has decided to impose a definitive anti-dumping duty, which is an additional tax on imports, on the product 1,4-butanediol. This chemical originates from China, Saudi Arabia, and the United States. This decision means that the provisional duties previously introduced are now becoming permanent.

The decision follows an investigation that began in June 2025. The investigation was initiated after the company INEOS Solvents SA complained that the import of this chemical was harming European producers. There were suspicions that the products were being sold in the EU at a price lower than their normal value, a practice known as dumping.

During the investigation, many large companies were given the opportunity to provide their input. Companies such as Wanhua Group, Sipchem, and Lyondell submitted responses and participated in discussions. Some companies argued that the investigation should not apply to all countries, but the Commission has decided to proceed with the decision.

The purpose of these duties is to create fair conditions for the industry within the EU. By imposing a tax on the cheaper imports, the aim is to ensure that European factories can compete on a level playing field without suffering economic damage.
